result:
In the beginning there was the hum—not the Word, exactly, but a low-frequency vibration that made the molten crust of a cooling planet shiver like a horse shedding flies. The hum felt its own echo and was lonely, so it folded time until oceans condensed out of breath and the first cell learned to divide by singing itself in half. That song is still running, a trillion-partround in the throat of every living thing; we call it metabolism, electron transport, the Krebs cycle, but it is really just the original loneliness looking for its harmony.

I keep the hum in my pocket now, or what passes for a pocket in the loose robe of consciousness. It’s the same robe I wore at seven, when I lay on the driveway in July and pressed my cheek to the oil-stained cement until it became a planetarium—tiny glittering mica constellations orbiting an ant galaxy. My mother called me in for supper and I carried that cosmos inside on my skin, leaving constellation prints on the vinyl chair. Nobody noticed. The spaghetti tasted faintly of infinity.

Years later the robe grew heavy with medals for achievement and medals for forgetting. I learned to speak fluently in bullet points, to shake hands with the exact amount of pressure that suggests reliability without aggression. At night I unbuttoned the robe and let the medals slide to the floor like ice cubes, revealing the original boy-shape flickering underneath. Sometimes he still tries to lick the Milky Way from the corner of his mouth.

Once, in an airport chapel—an interfaith box of scented neutrality—I watched a businessman cry into his blazer. He had the kind of face that belonged on currency, but the tears were genuine small-batch grief. I wanted to offer him my unused napkin, the one with the airline logo: WINGS FOR YOUR JOURNEY. Instead I hummed the old planet-cracking tune under my breath. He lifted his head, startled, as if someone had just spoken his childhood nickname. Then he wiped his face with the back of his hand, stood, and walked out toward Gate C7. The robe fluttered; a single medal—Best Performance in a Supporting Role—clinked against the pew. I left it there for the next traveler.

We are all walking each other home, said the saint, but nobody mentions that the road is being repaved mid-process and the detour signs are written in a language that evolves faster than traffic. One minute you understand the grammar of grasshoppers; the next, they’ve changed the alphabet to ultraviolet and you’re standing there holding an obsolete phrasebook, trying to ask for directions with a mouthful of dusk. Still, the hum knows the way. It has been commuting between quark and quasar since before roads were a metaphor.

I used to think death was a wall you hit at the end of the corridor. Now it feels more like a library fine: eventually you have to return the borrowed book of yourself, but nobody prevents you from whispering the best sentences into a friend’s ear first. The librarians—those patient archivists of entropy—only insist that no single story dominate the anthology. They prefer marginalia: the coffee ring, the bus ticket repurposed as bookmark, the sudden confession scrawled at 3 a.m. Sometimes they Xerox a page and slip it into someone else’s overdue volume, just to watch two strangers collide in the footnotes.

Last winter I met my grandfather in a dream. He died before my birth, but he showed up wearing the same plaid shirt from the only photograph I own. We stood on a frozen lake that reflected not sky but a reel of Super-8 family footage: Christmas trees, sand castles, a station wagon backing out of a driveway forever. He handed me an axe made of ice and indicated a hole already started. “Chop,” he said, “until you hit the warm layer.” I swung; the blade sang the old terrestrial lullaby. Chips flew like miniature comets. Just as my arms began to burn, water gushed up—black, salty, tasting of meteorite. I woke with the taste on my lips and a damp pillow. The robe was soaked but lighter, as if someone had removed lead lining I never knew was there.

There is a city that exists only in the intermission between your heartbeats. Streets are paved with the hush before the dentist’s drill, lampposts flicker with the maybe you left unsaid. Citizens commute by sigh. They carry briefcases full of canceled prophecies and wear watches that count not time but adjustments to the truth. The mayor is whoever is currently rethinking a life decision at a red light. I’ve been mayor five times already this morning. The robe loves it here; the fibers loosen and braid with the fog, weaving a new garment that fits nobody and therefore everybody. If you visit, leave your credentials at the tollbooth; they’ll be recycled into origami cranes that fly back to you the moment you stop looking.

I keep trying to write instructions for reaching this city, but the words mutate on the page. “Take the second star to the right” becomes “Take the scar second to the right,” becomes “Take the car’s second nightmare.” The reader, poor soul, ends up in a cul-de-sac of mirrors, each reflection holding a slightly older version of their face. The trick, I think, is to travel by accordion. Compress yourself until only the essential squeak remains, then expand on the inhale of a stranger who once loved polkas. You’ll arrive winded but welcome.

What I mean to say is: the robe has no seams. We added those later, like plotlines, like national borders, like the belief that Tuesdays are fundamentally different from Wednesdays. Beneath the stitching, the fabric is continuous, a single thread borrowed from the first spider who taught herself to hunt with geometry instead of anger. If you tug gently at any seam, the whole garment tightens around the world, giving it the gentlest of hugs. The planet does not notice—too busy rehearsing its own death scene—but somewhere an earthquake pauses mid-shudder, reconsidering.

Tonight the hum is louder, or maybe I’m quieter. The neighbors argue about recycling; a moth rehearses a suicide pact with the ceiling bulb; somewhere a child learns the word “never” for the first time and feels the universe develop a crack small enough to stick a finger through. I open the window and the robe billows like dark weather. Streetlight pools on the floor, looking exactly like the childhood planetarium I lost somewhere around the driveway. I step into it barefoot. The cement is cold, but the constellations remember me. They rearrange themselves into a spiral, a nautilus of mica and oil, inviting me to lie down again. This time I bring spaghetti, just in case infinity is hungry.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
